edges.description: NRAS mutations drive malignant transformation in congenital melanocytic nevi, with NRAS being the predominant genetic driver in large-giant CMNs.

evidence.snippet: The most exciting finding is the discovery of oncogenic BRAF mutations in both malignant melanoma and melanocytic nevi.

evidence.reference_title: The association of BRAF V600E gene mutation with proliferative activity and histopathological characteristics of congenital melanocytic nevi in children.
evidence.snippet: A lot of congenital melanocytic nevi (CMN) carry the somatic mutation in the oncogene BRAF V600E.

evidence.reference_title: Does the gene matter? Genotype-phenotype and genotype-outcome associations in congenital melanocytic naevi.
evidence.snippet: BRAF is confirmed as a much rarer cause of multiple CMN.

evidence.reference_title: Association of novel MUC16, MAP3K15 and ABCA1 mutation with giant congenital melanocytic nevus.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in NRAS have been previously detected in GCMN, but mutations in BRAF are generally lacking in the Chinese population.

nodes.description: Damage to DNA structure from various causes

Post-zygotic mosaic mutations in NRAS (codon 61) and BRAF (V600E) occur during embryogenesis in neural crest-derived melanocyte precursors, driving constitutive MAPK signaling. NRAS mutations are dominant in large/giant CMN (68-94%), while BRAF mutations are less common. Progression to melanoma requires additional alterations including TERT activation, CDKN2A loss, TP53 mutations, and PTEN inactivation.

evidence.snippet: The most exciting finding is the discovery of oncogenic BRAF mutations in both malignant melanoma and melanocytic nevi. This finding indicates that activation of the mitogen-activated protein kinase pathway may be a critical initiating step of melanocytic neoplasia.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Melanocytic Nevus Syndrome: A Case Series.
evidence.snippet: Congenital melanocytic nevus syndrome (CMNS) is the result of an abnormal proliferation of melanocytes in the skin and central nervous system caused by progenitor-cell mutations during embryonic development. Mutations in the NRAS gene have been detected in many of these cells.
Melanomas developing on pre-existing congenital or acquired nevi are usually of the superficial spreading subtype and harbor the BRAFV600E mutation.

evidence.reference_title: Does the gene matter? Genotype-phenotype and genotype-outcome associations in congenital melanocytic naevi.
evidence.snippet: NRAS mosaicism was found in 68%, BRAF in 7% and double wild-type in 25% of cases of CMN. NRAS was the commonest mutation in all sizes of CMN.

evidence.reference_title: NRAS mutant melanoma arising in a giant congenital melanocytic nevus in an infant.
evidence.snippet: NRAS mutant melanoma arising in a giant congenital melanocytic nevus in an infant.

evidence.reference_title: Does the gene matter? Genotype-phenotype and genotype-outcome associations in congenital melanocytic naevi.
evidence.snippet: Multiple congenital melanocytic naevi (CMN) have been shown to be caused by NRAS mosaic mutations in 70-80% of cases.

nodes.description: Compensatory activation of the PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway occurs alongside MAPK signaling, often driven by PTEN loss. This pathway cross-talk promotes cell survival, invasion, and resistance to MEK inhibition. Combined targeting of both MAPK and PI3K pathways represents a rational therapeutic strategy.
Ultraviolet (UV) radiation can damage the DNA in skin cells, contributing to mutations and the development of melanoma.

evidence.reference_title: The genomic landscape of childhood and adolescent melanoma.
evidence.snippet: the three CNMs contained an activating NRAS Q61 mutation and no TERT-p mutations.
validated the role of ultraviolet-induced DNA mutations in melanoma formation
